name: DexCom, Inc.

profile.description: DexCom, Inc. designs, develops, and commercializes continuous glucose monitoring (CGM) systems for people with diabetes and those seeking metabolic health insights. Products include the Dexcom G7 (prescription CGM for diabetes management) and Stelo (over-the-counter biosensor for general wellness). Founded in 1999 and headquartered in San Diego, California, DexCom has pioneered CGM technology for over 25 years.

innovation.label: STRONG
innovation.analysis: Track Record (2/3): DexCom launched the Stelo biosensor in 2024 as its first over-the-counter CGM product, dramatically expanding the addressable market beyond insulin-dependent diabetics to the 100M+ Americans with prediabetic conditions [1]. The G7 system launched in 2023 represented a 60% smaller form factor with direct-to-watch connectivity. However, the core business model remains CGM sensors — no transformative pivot on the scale of entering an entirely new category. AI Deployment (1/2): DexCom has deployed AI-powered Smart Food Logging that uses image recognition to estimate nutritional content and correlate with glucose response, and the Stelo app integrates Google AI for personalized metabolic insights [2]. The company published formal AI Principles governing its use of artificial intelligence. No specific AI revenue figures are quantified separately. Market Share (2/2): DexCom is the dominant CGM platform with approximately 35-40% global CGM market share and category leadership in the U.S. prescription CGM segment. The G7 earned integration partnerships with Insulet Omnipod 5, NovoPen 6, and Oura Ring, signaling ecosystem dominance [1][3]. Product Pipeline (1/2): The reimagined Stelo app experience is launching in 2026, Smart Basal received FDA clearance in November 2025 enabling insulin therapy integration, and the pipeline includes extended sensor durations and implantable long-term sensors [3]. Specific next-gen product launch dates beyond the Stelo app are not publicly committed. R&D Intensity (1/1): R&D spending of $157.5M in Q3 2025 alone represents approximately 13-14% of revenue, well above medtech industry average of 6-8% [3].

inspiration.label: DEVELOPING
inspiration.analysis: Mission Floor (3/4): DexCom articulates its purpose as empowering people to take control of their health through innovative biosensing technology. This is specific and thoughtful — it connects the company's CGM technology to a larger purpose of health empowerment and has been consistent since 1999. The expansion into Stelo for general metabolic wellness demonstrates strategic decisions tracing back to this purpose. It falls short of a 4 because the purpose, while genuine, has not yet been tested through the kind of multi-decade leadership transitions that validate a transformative Just Cause [2]. Community Impact (1/2): DexCom makes CGM technology accessible through patient assistance programs and has worked to expand insurance coverage for CGM. The company has supported diabetes advocacy organizations and community health initiatives, but no distinctive, large-scale philanthropic program with specific dollar amounts was identified [4]. Employee Retention (1/2): DexCom has approximately 10,000 employees with generally stable leadership. The appointment of high-profile board members like Rick Osterloh (Google SVP) and Dr. Euan Ashley (Stanford Chair of Medicine) in 2025-2026 signals an attractive corporate culture for top talent [2]. No specific turnover or tenure data is publicly available. Insider Buying (1/2): CEO Jacob Leach purchased $1.0M worth of shares at $55.04 in November 2025, representing meaningful conviction. Net insider activity shows $924K more bought than sold in the last 12 months [3].

infrastructure.label: STRONG
infrastructure.analysis: Mechanically scored on available metrics for a Growth stock. DexCom demonstrates exceptional financial health across nearly all dimensions. D/E of 47% is well below the 70% target, indicating conservative leverage with net cash of $1.03B [4]. ROE of 35.62% far exceeds the 15% target, reflecting efficient capital deployment. Revenue growth of 15% YoY in Q1 2026 exceeds the 10% target, and 5-year revenue CAGR of approximately 19.4% (from $1.925B in 2020 to $4.662B in 2025) is nearly double the target [1]. Forward P/E of 26.21 is above the 20 target but scores 0.845, reflecting reasonable premium for a high-growth medtech name. Profit margin of 19.31% is just below the 20% target (0.966 score). Forward PEG of 1.19 scores 0.905, indicating attractive growth-adjusted valuation. All metric scores are at or near maximum. Liquidity floor easily cleared at $392M+ daily dollar volume [4].

ai_resilience.label: LOW RISK
ai_resilience.analysis: AI Risk Protection (7/9): DexCom's core business is manufacturing and selling physical biosensors that are FDA-regulated Class II medical devices — AI structurally cannot replace the need for a physical sensor adhered to the body that measures interstitial glucose via electrochemistry [5]. The CGM market is protected by deep regulatory moats (FDA 510(k) clearance, CE marking), switching costs (insulin pump integrations, physician prescribing habits), and proprietary sensor chemistry developed over 25+ years. Analysts specifically note that DexCom is "gaining from AI, not disrupted by it" with AI integration into CGM platforms actually increasing the value proposition and expanding the addressable market [5]. The physical-world nature of glucose monitoring — requiring hardware on the body — makes this business structurally immune to AI substitution. The one point deducted reflects theoretical risk from AI-enabled competitors (Apple, Samsung) potentially entering biometric sensing, though none have achieved FDA clearance for glucose monitoring. AI Opportunity (1/1): DexCom generates meaningful AI-related revenue through its Stelo biosensor built with Google AI, AI-powered Smart Food Logging, and Smart Basal FDA-cleared insulin therapy integration. These AI features are shipping to customers and generating revenue, differentiating DexCom's CGM platform from competitors [2][3].

infinite_game.summary: DexCom under CEO Kevin Sayer (2017-2024) and now Jacob Leach demonstrates meaningful infinite game characteristics. The company has maintained a consistent purpose of empowering health through biosensing for 25+ years and has made courageous bets expanding beyond its established Type 1 diabetes base into the massive metabolic wellness market via Stelo. However, the company is still relatively young as a public company and has not yet been tested through the kind of existential crisis or leadership transition that fully validates infinite game thinking.

just_cause.analysis: Just Cause (2/3): DexCom's purpose of empowering people to take control of their health through innovative biosensing is genuine, distinctive, and has been consistently pursued since the company's founding in 1999. The expansion from Type 1 diabetes to Type 2 to general metabolic wellness via Stelo represents strategic decisions that clearly trace back to this purpose — making glucose visibility accessible to everyone, not just insulin-dependent patients [1][2]. Leadership references this purpose regularly. However, the company is only 27 years old and has undergone its first CEO transition recently (Sayer to Leach), so the cause has not yet been validated through the kind of multi-decade, multi-crisis consistency that earns a 3.

trusting_teams.analysis: Trusting Teams (1/2): DexCom maintains a stable organization of approximately 10,000 employees with consistent leadership. The recruitment of board members like Rick Osterloh (Google SVP) and Dr. Euan Ashley (Stanford) suggests an organization that attracts and retains high-caliber talent [2]. No evidence of significant layoffs, cultural dysfunction, or organizational instability was identified. However, no specific data on employee tenure, voluntary turnover, or cultural practices that would indicate exceptional trust was found.

worthy_rivals.analysis: Worthy Rivals (1/1): DexCom consistently acknowledges Abbott's FreeStyle Libre as a worthy competitor and uses the competitive pressure to improve — the G7's smaller form factor and Stelo's OTC positioning were both developed in response to Abbott's aggressive pricing and market expansion strategy. Management commentary focuses on expanding the CGM market rather than destroying Abbott, framing the competitive dynamic as growing the category rather than fighting for fixed share [1].

existential_flexibility.analysis: Existential Flexibility (1/2): DexCom has meaningfully evolved its business model by expanding from prescription-only CGM for Type 1 diabetics to the over-the-counter Stelo biosensor targeting the massive metabolic wellness market [1][3]. This represents entering an adjacent market with a new distribution model (DTC, no prescription required), which required significant strategic and regulatory adaptation. The Smart Basal FDA clearance expands into insulin therapy integration. However, this is incremental evolution of the core CGM platform, not the kind of transformative reinvention (abandoning a core business) that earns a 2.

courage_to_lead.analysis: Courage to Lead (1/2): DexCom's launch of Stelo as an OTC product at a significantly lower price point than its prescription G7 demonstrated willingness to cannibalize its own higher-margin business to expand the addressable market — a genuinely courageous decision that prioritized long-term market expansion over short-term ASP protection [1][3]. The company also invested heavily in R&D (13-14% of revenue) during a period when the stock was under significant pressure following a disappointing Q2 2024 guidance cut. However, these decisions, while meaningful, are not the pattern of repeatedly costly long-term bets that earns a 2.

investment_thesis: DexCom delivered Q1 2026 revenue of $1.192B (up 15% YoY) with GAAP net income of $199.5M and EPS of $0.51, beating consensus estimates by 17% [1]. Full-year 2026 guidance of $5.16-5.25B implies 11-13% growth with continued margin expansion — profit margin has improved from 10% to 17% YoY in Q1. The balance sheet is pristine with net cash of $1.03B and D/E of just 47%. At a forward PEG of 1.19 and forward P/E of 26x, the stock is attractively valued for a medtech leader with 19%+ 5-year revenue CAGR, 35.6% ROE, and a $100B+ TAM expansion opportunity via Stelo targeting 100M+ Americans with prediabetic conditions [4]. The AI-powered Smart Food Logging and Smart Basal integration create platform stickiness that differentiates DexCom from commodity sensor competitors.
thesis_continued: The primary risk is the stock's steep decline from its 2024 highs above $130 to approximately $70 today, reflecting market skepticism about DexCom's ability to execute the Stelo-driven TAM expansion while maintaining margins. The Q2 2024 guidance cut damaged management credibility, and the recent CEO transition from Kevin Sayer to Jacob Leach introduces execution risk during a critical growth phase [3]. Competitive pressure from Abbott's FreeStyle Libre (which holds majority global CGM share at lower price points) and potential entry from big tech companies into biometric sensing could compress margins. The class action lawsuit related to G7 CGM connectivity issues and two FDA recalls of Dexcom mobile apps in 2025-2026 highlight product quality risks that could erode the brand trust that underpins DexCom's premium pricing [5]. Revenue growth deceleration from 24% in 2023 to 15% in Q1 2026 may continue if Stelo adoption falls short of expectations.
key_catalysts: Reimagined Stelo app launching in 2026 with Google AI-powered personalized metabolic insights, targeting the 100M+ American prediabetic market and potentially transforming DexCom from a diabetes device company into a metabolic health platform

Smart Basal FDA clearance (November 2025) enabling integration with insulin therapy, opening a new revenue stream in connected insulin delivery and strengthening the closed-loop artificial pancreas ecosystem

Operating margin expansion continuing as the company scales — profit margin improved from 10% to 17% YoY in Q1 2026, with management targeting further leverage on the existing manufacturing and distribution infrastructure
key_risks: CEO transition from Kevin Sayer to Jacob Leach introduces leadership execution risk during a critical growth phase, compounded by damaged credibility from the Q2 2024 guidance miss that sent shares down 40%

Abbott FreeStyle Libre holds majority global CGM market share at lower price points, and potential big tech entrants (Apple, Samsung) into non-invasive glucose sensing could commoditize the CGM category over the medium term

Two FDA recalls of Dexcom mobile apps and a class action lawsuit over G7 connectivity issues highlight product quality risks that could erode the brand trust essential to DexCom's premium pricing strategy
